items.q: Why is high IQ a problem for the statistical infrequency definition?
items.correct: It is statistically rare but not abnormal/undesirable.
items.misconception: Because high IQ is actually common.
items.why: Students miss that statistical infrequency lacks a value judgement - it just measures rarity. High IQ is rare but desirable, exposing the definition's limitation.

items.q: What are the three elements of Beck's negative triad?
items.correct: Negative views of self, world, and future.
items.misconception: Negative views of past, present, and future.
items.why: Students often substitute 'past' for 'self' or 'world'. The triad is about the DOMAINS of negative thought (self/world/future), not time periods.
Students reverse IV and DV. The IV is what you CHANGE (input); the DV is what you MEASURE (outcome). Mnemonic: 'I change the I.'

items.q: Which measure is least affected by extreme outliers?
items.why: Correct answer: Median. “Mean” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In a normal distribution what percentage of scores lie within ±2 SD?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 95%. “68%” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In a normal distribution approximately what percent of scores lie within ±1 SD of the mean?
items.why: The real figure is 68. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
